title: Rockaway Beach Boardwalk
description: A boy stands on the boardwalk at Beach 81st Street, in front of a row of shuttered storefronts: a grocery, candy and cigar store, and luncheonettes.
location_note: Approximate — placed at Beach 81th Street, mentioned in the record.

title: Rockaway Beach Boardwalk
description: A view along the boardwalk looking northwest from Beach 80th Street (now de-mapped at the boardwalk.) The boarded-up businesses include an ice cream shop, several small shops without signage, the Coffee Pot luncheonette, and the Beer Garden Restaurant at the northeast corner of Beach 81st Street. Magyary's Luncheonette, on the northwest corner, is seen at far left. The peaked-roof buildings behind the shops are rental bungalows. A view of this block taken from Beach 81st Street can be seen in record psc-000389.
location_note: Approximate — placed at Beach 80th Street, mentioned in the record.

title: Rockaway Beach Boulevard
description: View of Rockaway Beach Boulevard from the corner of Beach 114th Street.
location_note: Approximate — placed at Beach 114th Street, mentioned in the record.

title: Rockaway Beach Boardwalk
description: A view along the boardwalk looking northeast from Beach 81st Street. The boarded-up businesses include the Beer Garden Restaurant, the Coffee Pot luncheonette, and an ice cream shop near at the far right, near Beach 80th Street (now de-mapped at the boardwalk.) The peaked-roof buildings behind the shops are rental bungalows. A tripod is set up on the boardwalk in front of the restaurant.
location_note: Approximate — placed at Beach 81th Street, mentioned in the record.

title: Beach 80th Street in Rockaway Beach
description: The view from the boardwalk looking north along Beach 80th Street (now de-mapped.) The large building on the right is the Sea Breeze Hotel. The sign on the hotel porch advertises the 80th Street Hot Ocean Baths.
location_note: Approximate — placed at Beach 80th Street, mentioned in the record.

title: Beach 78th Street in Rockaway Beach
description: Men with shovels and wheelbarrows work in sand piled up to the left of the boardwalk ramp in this view north along Beach 78th Street (now de-mapped here). The sign on the building at far right advertises "furnished or unfurnished houses 9 to 16 rooms" and "apartments 1 to 4 rooms to let by month or season."
location_note: Approximate — placed at Beach 78th Street, mentioned in the record.

title: Beach 79th Street in Rockaway Beach
description: View north along Beach 79th Street (now de-mapped here) from the ocean front boardwalk. The sign at left advertises the vacant lots as being for sale or lease.
location_note: Approximate — placed at Beach 79th Street, mentioned in the record.

title: House in Rockaway Beach

title: House in Rockaway Beach

location_note: Approximate — placed at Beach 77th Street, mentioned in the record.

title: Rockaway Beach Blvd Sign
description: The Rockaways were damaged by Hurricane Sandy but survived after the storm passed. The street sign stands a a symbol of the resiliance of residents and homes that weathered the storm. [From Adam: I come from Poland, from Warsaw, soon after I finished high school I decided to go to U.S. for the first time. NYC was the place where I started my first photographic adventure. That's why at the beginning I focused mainly on urban photography, later in Poland I also photographed the industrial sites of the cities, but through those years was exploring more and more natural landscapes, and today here on Rockaway the most important for me became light, open space and atmosphere. In my art I aim to catch the place, people, situation, weather in that very moment of seeing more then usually. Before I moved to Rockaway I was living in Brooklyn for over four years. I was a witness of two biggest hurricanes in the last years, Irine and Sandy, but Sandy was the one that really changed everything around and within me. By the time of hurricane I was in Brooklyn, I came to Rockaway the day after and couldn't believe what I saw. Unfortunately I forgot to take camera with me, so next day I came there again with my camera and started document the whole disaster. I’ve been always very familiar with Rockaway and Broad Channel. Eventually I moved here two months after the hurricane, which I planned before anyway. I became a member of RAA (Rockaway Artist Alliance) and participant of three exhibitions at the gallery T7 in Fort Tilden. So far so good.]
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: Rockaway Beach after Hurricane Sandy
description: Christine Martinez and her neighbors on Beach 91st Street set up this cooking stove on their street in the weeks after Hurricane Sandy. Their fires at night would both cook their food and also provide a gathering spot on the block where they could discuss their recovery efforts. According to Martinez, "we came up with this idea to put this stove in the middle of the street to let people know on our block that there is warmth and food available."
location_note: Approximate — placed at Beach 91th Street, mentioned in the record.

title: Railroad Crossing on Rockaway Boulevard
description: A view east along Rockaway Boulevard across the Long Island Rail Road's Rockaway Beach Division tracks, taken from a point about one hundred yards west of the track. Elimination of the grade crossing was ordered in 1927. The factory building on the right held the Quartin Company, a manufacturer of "artistic paper boxes" according to the signs on the building and the nearby water tower.
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: Looking Up Ocean Promenade Walkway
description: This was the view of the Rockaway Boardwalk in Rockaway Park near B116th street after Hurricane Sandy hit. Entire chunks of the wooden boardwalk were pushed dozens of feet into buildings, or simply collapsed from the pressure of the water.

title: Media Comes to Report the Damage

title: Atlantic Avenue in Richmond Hill
description: A view looking east along the north side of Atlantic Avenue from the platform of the Long Island Rail Road's Rockaway Division line at 100th Street. Street. The low brick building on the left houses the New York & Queens Electric Light & Power Company. A train can be seen on the grade-level tracks of the Long Island Rail Road's Atlantic Avenue line, which ran down the center of Atlantic Avenue. Richmond Hill is on the left, and Ozone Park is on the right.

title: Fishing at Hammels Drawbridge

title: Bathing in the Breakers at Hammels
description: This postcard depicts beachgoers "bathing" in the ocean by holding on to ropes attached to poles and buoys. In the early 20th century, the Hammels community was a popular Rockaway seaside destination; it had its own stop on the Long Island Rail Road until 1941. The hand-written message on the reverse of the card reads: "Dear Cousin Olga: - You can expect Herman at 403 P.M. Sunday. Have not heard from you yet to my last letter. Your Cousin Lu." It is postmarked July 10, 1908, from Brooklyn, N.Y.
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: No Room For More
description: A bag of garbage sits outside a compactor storage room that has been locked because it is already full. The photo was taken to illustrate the poor living conditions in the apartment building at 23-08 Mott Avenue but was not published.
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: Disgraceful Conditions
description: Tenant Carolyn Reeves tries to open the first-floor door to the broken compactor, but has to hold back the overflowing garbage outside. The photo was used to illustrate an article on the poor living conditions in the apartment building, at 23-08 Mott Avenue.
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: Garbage at Curb
description: The neatly-stacked garbage cans in front of the apartment building across the street stand in stark constrast to the unsightly piles deposited on the street outside 23-08 Mott Avenue.
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: Burned-Out Apartment
description: View of burned-out windows in a second-floor apartment at 23-08 Mott Avenue.
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: Unrepaired Fire Damage
description: Tenants Adele Gregory (left) and Rosemary Gibbs (holding her infant son Eugene) point out roof damage from a fire that occurred seven months earlier and had not yet been repaired. Photo was used to illustrate an article on the poor living conditions in the apartment building, at 23-08 Mott Avenue.
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: Long Island Rail Road Junction at Valley Stream
description: Looking south across the Long Island Rail Road tracks at Valley Stream, near the junction of the Montauk and Far Rockaway branches.
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: Hotel and Pavilion
description: A photograph of an unidentified hotel and pavilion, taken at a considerable distance from the shore of what may be Far Rockaway.
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: The Future Seagirt Village
description: Three unidentified men stand next to an architect's drawing of the Seagirt Village housing development. The development was built in 1966 by Jack Singer, and he may be the man seen at far right
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: House and Sheds, Jamaica
description: Looking west from Rockaway Boulevard along the line of the proposed North Conduit Avenue. A man is seen standing on the road embankment left of center. Buildings along this line were to be demolished. The building partly visible at far right is 154-18 Rockaway Boulevard.
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: Houses Near Boardwalk
description: A rear view of two wood-frame houses on Beach 82nd Street, taken from Beach 83rd Street (both de-mapped). The ocean front boardwalk is at the far right. On the wooden slat fence is a movie poster advertising films at the RKO Columbia Theatre, which was located at 1014 Beach 20th Street in Far Rockaway.
location_note: Approximate — placed at Beach 82th Street, mentioned in the record.

title: Valley Stream Railroad Station
description: A group of men and boys stand outside the railroad station. The tracks in front of them are those of the Long Island Rail Road's Montauk branch; the train in the background, behind the station, is on the Far Rockaway branch tracks.
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: "Making of the Panorama" Exhibition
description: Marc Miller, Curator, and Donna Tuman, Curator of Education, examine the never-installed Far Rockaway section of the Panorama during "The Making of the Panorama of the City of New York: An Exhibition Documenting the History of the World's Largest Scale Model" at the Queens Museum.
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: View West From 160th Street, Jamaica
description: Looking west from 160th Street along the line of the proposed North Conduit Avenue. At far left is New York State Route 27 (Sunrise Highway), with the Rockaway Boulevard overpass visible in the distance. The utility poles at right are on Southern Boulevard (now North Conduit Avenue.)
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: Train at Rockaway Junction
description: Four men pose with Engine No. 109 and Coach No. 314 outside the Rockaway Junction and Station in what is now Jamaica. The two conductors at right are identified as William Jerome and George Oxley. The man at far left is the fireman, John Tooker, and the man looking out of the locomotive's cab is the engineer, Hugh Livingstone. The typewritten caption is part of the image on the polyester negative, and it reads, "L.I.R.R. rapid transit trains seldom consisted of more than two cars at that time (1896). There were about thirty local stations then between Flatbush Ave., Brooklyn, and Rockaway Junction, many in Brooklyn were just open platforms. Engine 109. This picture was taken at Rockaway Junction Station (now Hillside L.I.) in the summer of 1896 by an itinerant photographer named Dill. Of the four men shown, John Tooker, fireman, Hugh Livingston Engineer, William Jerome and George Oxley, Conductors, only the fireman is now living in 1953, and he is 79 years old."
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: Railroad Crossing in Seaside
description: A man with a book in his hands stands on the tracks at right; another man is partly seen inside the small building at left in this view looking west along the Long Island Rail Road's Rockaway Beach branch. On the building is a sign that reads "Loafing Prohibited" and another above the door reading "Conway St.", the former name of Beach 105th St. A gas holder is in the distance at far right.
location_note: Approximate — placed at Beach 105th Street, mentioned in the record.

title: Street Corner in Ozone Park
description: A view looking southeast from North Conduit Avenue at its intersection with 101st Street. In the center background can be seen the Sunrise Highway Bridge over the Long Island Rail Road tracks. The grade-level crossing for the tracks of the Rockaway line can just be seen at the far left. Two automobiles are parked in a lot on the corner opposite. There is also a street sign, a mailbox, and a bus stop on the corner.
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.

title: Atlantic Avenue in Richmond Hill
description: A view looking west along the north side of Atlantic Avenue from a point near 108th Street. A row of storefronts and several attached homes are at right. The Long Island Rail Road's grade-level tracks, which ran down the center of Atlantic Avenue, are beyond the fence at left. There is a pedestrian trestle on the left, which allowed passage to the other side of Atlantic Avenue. The trestle of the Long Island Rail Road's Rockaway Line, at 99th Street, is visible in the far distance.
location_note: Approximate — no specific address in the record; placed at the Rockaway Beach neighborhood center.
